impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patentee's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patentee's unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the 'nonstaple goods' defense applies when the tied products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under federal antitrust law. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers exclusively purchasing Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the jury's finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's 'nonstaple goods' defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors can challenge patent holders who leverage patent rights to force exclusive purchasing of unpatented products, ensuring fair competition in adjacent product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face significant antitrust liability risk when structuring license agreements that require customers to purchase unpatented products exclusively, even when those products are related to the patented technology.

impacts.caseName: Teva Pharmaceuticals International GmbH v. Eli Lilly and Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a method of treating headache using humanized anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies were invalid for failure to satisfy the written-description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112, where the specification disclosed only one such antibody but the claims covered a genus.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's grant of JMOL invalidating Teva's headache treatment patents covering anti-CGRP antagonist antibodies. The court found that the specification's disclosure of one humanized antibody, combined with knowledge of prior-art methods for making and humanizing anti-CGRP antibodies, was sufficient under both the written-description and enablement requirements. This reinstated a jury verdict of willful infringement by Lilly.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical and biotech patent holders benefit from a more favorable standard for genus claims, as disclosing one species combined with known prior-art methods may satisfy written description and enablement for broader claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Generic and biosimilar manufacturers face broader patent exclusivity for biologic drug claims, potentially delaying market entry where genus claims covering therapeutic antibodies are upheld.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing emulated function calls to a model are directed to an abstract idea under Alice step one, and whether damages for patent infringement may include sales to customers outside the United States.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million infringement judgment, holding that Columbia's cybersecurity patents claiming methods for detecting anomalous program executions were directed to an abstract idea under Alice step one, requiring remand for step two analysis. The court also ruled that the district court erred in allowing damages based on Norton's foreign sales, and vacated enhanced damages and attorneys' fees that rested partly on an improper contempt finding.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies benefit from the court's reinforcement that software patents claiming generalized detection-by-comparison methods face rigorous Alice scrutiny, and that extraterritorial damages require stronger justification.
impacts.negativeImplications: University and corporate patent holders asserting cybersecurity patents face heightened invalidation risk under § 101, and cannot easily recover damages based on the accused infringer's foreign sales.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a wireless device's capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's streaming media patents claiming server-side tailoring of content specifications to device capabilities were ineligible under § 101. The court reversed the district court's judgment awarding GoTV $2.5 million and entered judgment for Netflix, finding the claims directed to the abstract idea of adapting content presentation to device characteristics.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies gain stronger defenses against patent assertions based on generalized content-adaptation concepts, reducing exposure to PAE (patent assertion entity) litigation.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the content delivery and streaming technology space face greater invalidity risk under § 101 for claims directed to server-side content optimization and device-tailored delivery.

impacts.caseName: International Medical Devices, Inc. v. Cornell
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the plaintiff presented legally sufficient evidence that each of its four asserted trade secrets was protectable under California law, and whether ideas that are generally known can constitute an inventive contribution for patent inventorship purposes.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the denial of JMOL on trade secret misappropriation claims, finding insufficient evidence that the asserted trade secrets related to a cosmetic penile implant were actually protectable because the underlying information was generally known. The court also reversed the patent invalidity determination, holding that contributions of generally known ideas cannot support a finding of inventive contribution for inventorship correction. The counterfeiting verdict and damages were affirmed.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses accused of trade secret misappropriation benefit from the court's rigorous requirement that plaintiffs prove each asserted trade secret is not generally known, raising the evidentiary bar for such claims.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trade secret owners face a heightened burden to demonstrate that their asserted secrets contain genuinely novel, non-public information rather than information that is generally known in the relevant field.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent infringement claims under 35 U.S.C. § 271(a) can reach multi-core processor designs where the accused acts of making and testing occur outside the United States, and whether doctrine of equivalents can overcome literal noninfringement of specific claim limitations.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed-in-part the district court's grant of summary judgment of noninfringement for Intel on extraterritoriality grounds, finding genuine disputes of material fact as to whether Intel's activities constituted domestic infringement. The court affirmed the rejection of VLSI's doctrine of equivalents theory on a separate claim limitation. The case was remanded for further proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting claims against semiconductor and technology companies benefit from the court's willingness to find triable issues on domestic infringement even when significant design and manufacturing activities occur abroad.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies with global manufacturing operations face continued uncertainty about extraterritorial reach of U.S. patent infringement claims when some domestic activities are involved.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process fraud claim alleging attempted monopolization through enforcement of a patent obtained by fraud.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgments on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust claims involving coiled tubing patents. The court found genuine factual disputes about whether Tenaris withheld material prior art (the CYMAX Documents) during patent prosecution with intent to deceive, and whether Global Tubing could establish the elements of Walker Process fraud for its attempted monopolization claim.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies defending against patents potentially procured through inequitable conduct benefit from the court's insistence that factual disputes about intent to deceive and materiality must go to trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face trial exposure on inequitable conduct and potential antitrust liability under Walker Process where there is evidence of withheld prior art, even where summary judgment had been granted in their favor.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative may testify at summary judgment about facts outside their personal knowledge, and whether computer source code constitutes hearsay for purposes of proving a patent invalidity defense under the on-sale bar.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of invalidity under the pre-AIA on-sale bar. The court held that a 30(b)(6) corporate witness may testify beyond personal knowledge at summary judgment, and that computer source code (as opposed to descriptive comments within code) is not hearsay because it constitutes machine instructions rather than human assertions. These evidentiary rulings permitted Powerteq to establish that a prior-art device was sold more than one year before the patent's priority date.
impacts.positiveImplications: Defendants challenging patent validity benefit from broader admissibility of corporate representative testimony and source code evidence, making it easier to prove prior art sales and public use.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased invalidation risk as corporate witnesses can now testify about historical company sales practices beyond their personal knowledge, and source code is admissible as non-hearsay evidence of prior art functionality.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action after having missed the statutory deadline for seeking a stay of the original action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action under § 1659(a)(2) because BioMarin had previously missed the 30-day deadline for seeking a stay of the original action. The court rejected the strategy of dismissing and refiling a declaratory judgment complaint to reset the statutory clock for a mandatory stay request.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical companies filing declaratory judgment actions are protected from opponents' attempts to manipulate mandatory stay provisions by dismissing and refiling related ITC proceedings or district court complaints.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the 30-day mandatory stay deadline cannot cure the deficiency by engineering a refiled action, limiting their ability to defer district court proceedings pending ITC determinations.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple Watch models incorporating blood oxygen measurement technology infringed Masimo's wearable pulse oximetry patents and whether the ITC's domestic industry findings and validity rulings were proper.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's determination that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watches that infringed Masimo's patents covering wearable blood oxygen measurement devices. The court upheld the ITC's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, and infringement findings, sustaining the limited exclusion order barring importation of the infringing products.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with domestic industry investments in wearable medical technology can effectively enforce their IP rights through the ITC, obtaining exclusion orders that bar importation of infringing consumer electronics.
impacts.negativeImplications: Major consumer electronics companies face significant import bans when ITC exclusion orders are affirmed, potentially requiring product redesigns or licensing agreements to continue selling key product lines in the U.S.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and then granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft. The court found the district court improperly excluded the expert's opinions and erred in denying Exafer's motion to reopen discovery to cure any deficiencies. The case was remanded, preserving Exafer's ability to prove damages for alleged infringement of its network optimization patents by Microsoft's Azure platform.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from the court's insistence that damages expert reports not be excluded without proper justification, and that courts should consider allowing supplemental discovery rather than granting summary judgment for absence of a remedy.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers cannot rely on aggressive Daubert motions to eliminate damages evidence and then obtain summary judgment solely on the theory that no remedy exists, as courts must more carefully evaluate the underlying expert opinions.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Patent Trial and Appeal Board correctly construed the term 'encryption information' in claims directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the challenged claims would have been obvious under the corrected construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction of 'encryption information' in DivX's streaming media patent and vacated the Board's decision rejecting Netflix's obviousness challenge. The court adopted a broader construction and remanded for the Board to reassess obviousness under the corrected claim scope, potentially invalidating the patent claims at issue.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming services and technology companies challenging competitors' patents in IPR proceedings benefit from the court's willingness to correct overly narrow Board claim constructions that shielded claims from prior art.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims in the digital media streaming space face renewed invalidity challenges when the Federal Circuit broadens claim constructions adopted by the Board.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in countervailing duty and antidumping investigations is compatible with the governing statute and whether it abrogates the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in trade investigations is incompatible with the statute and does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial records. The court rejected the ITC's mandamus petition seeking to preserve its confidentiality practices.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers, domestic manufacturers, and the public gain greater transparency in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings, with access to information that may have been unnecessarily shielded from disclosure.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies submitting questionnaire responses in trade investigations face reduced confidentiality protections, as the ITC can no longer automatically designate all responses as confidential without a case-by-case determination that the information warrants such treatment.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding the Board's exercise of discretionary authority to deny IPR institution are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the Director retains ultimate authority over non-institution decisions, and the instructions do not bind the Director.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's broad discretion to decline IPR institution without being constrained by formal rulemaking procedures, preserving flexibility in patent validity challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and patent challengers face continued uncertainty regarding IPR institution criteria, as the PTO can adjust its discretionary denial policies through informal guidance without public notice-and-comment procedures.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund unrelated court technology projects was properly approved, including the adequacy of class representation and propriety of incentive awards.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's approval of a $125 million class settlement resolving claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees beyond what was necessary to operate the electronic records system. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees awards, and incentive awards to the three nonprofit class representatives, rejecting a pro se objector's challenges.
impacts.positiveImplications: Law firms and businesses that are frequent PACER users will receive refunds for excess fees paid during the eight-year class period, and the ruling validates that excessive government user fees can be challenged through class action litigation.
